result:
The first thing you notice about a quiet house at three in the morning is the small army of sounds that have actually been standing at attention all along, waiting patiently for the volume knob on the rest of the world to drop. The refrigerator hums louder than city traffic and the radiator sighs like a bored commuter. Somewhere in the walls something scrapes—tiny claws reminding you that you are never truly alone, even in suburbia. With the lights off you can see the small red eyes of electronics scattered around the room, blinking at you like sleepy dwarfs sharing a secret joke. You discover your own heartbeat is a kind of uncomfortable guest, pushing blood around in small off-beat flourishes, as if it practiced this performance just for this very hour.

I have been up for the last few nights chasing what I call “the real bibliography” of one Giuseppe Dolori, a minor nineteenth-century ceramist from Faenza whose work nobody remembers. I do not remember how I first encountered his name. Perhaps the word “dolori” (“pain”) lodged itself in the soft shell of my tired brain like grit in an oyster. In any case I now have seventeen brown-paper packages stacked on the dining table, each one labeled “Dolori” in tired ballpoint pen. They contain auction catalogs, fragile Private-Library bookplates, and postcards whose Italian stamps went extinct decades ago. Nothing here amounts to proof of genius, only proof of stubbornness: records of small-town exhibitions listed next to wheat prices; a single critical note—one column inch—in La Gazzetta di Bologna. Finding talent in the footnotes of footnotes is the kind of scavenger hunt that keeps insomniacs alive.

Last shipment was different, however. Beneath layers of yellowed invoice tissue I found a shard of pottery no larger than a thumbnail. The glaze—Persian blue with a micro-fracture running like lightning—bears a mark underneath dizioin, “perdono.” Forgiveness. The ache inside that syllable feels dangerously contemporary. Holding the shard against my own heartbeat makes the two pulses argue quietly. I cannot decide which of us insists more strongly on the future.

There is a way memories orbit around objects. A blue scarf becomes a planet with rings of laughter and spilled wine. My mother’s passport photo from 1987 retains the carnival glow of a safe trip home. The shard is already gathering a tiny solar system: the voice of my ex lover who once told me Italians have seventy-four words for grief; the auric smell of Earl Grey made at 4:00 a.m. by someone no longer alive; the click of a bicycle freewheel on Via Mascarella during my lonely study year. The memory-nodes cling, accumulating mass, bending time. Holding the shard now is almost painful, the same way holding one’s child can be—beauty weighing precisely as much as terror.

I begin speaking aloud to Giuseppe. I ask him what failure feels like when it lasts a lifetime. His reply is long and predictably ceramic: silence fired at nine hundred and fifty degrees. I try again: “Did you smash this piece yourself or did the kiln collapse in solidarity?” The refrigerator answers instead, clunking through a defrost cycle like any good stagehand who has grown tired of the actors. At 5:12 a.m. the sky bruises pale purple outside the kitchen window and I realize I have misplaced an entire night inside these conversations, the way one buttonholes a stranger on a train and forgets stations.

When the horizon turns coral, I take the shard to the windowsill. Light passes through the crack and paints a hairline orange river on the wall, shrinking as the sun climbs. The image is so fragile it vanishes between blinks. I keep my eyes open until they water; this is the only heroism still available at dawn. An early garbage truck grinds gears. Somewhere a neighbor’s dog attempts scales, off key. The city is rebooting: kettle hiss, subway rumble, underfed pigeons challenging gravity. I stand naked between yesterday’s pajamas and today’s obligations, holding a sliver of baked earth, suddenly aware that I have never visited Faenza in my life.

I booked the ticket while brushing my teeth. The airline homepage had that implacable cheer of a corporate smiley: Welcome Luigi! it lied. My name is not Luigi. My toothbrush buzzed, foam sliding like whipped guilt across my chin. Confirmation email dinged in before I rinsed. Five nights in an Airbnb above a bakery. The host’s avatar shows a nonna who raises one eyebrow in knowing skepticism, as if the room comes pre-haunted. I have 189 euros left in the travel fund I keep in a chipped teapot labeled “for imaginary destinations.” Faenza is no longer imaginary after the mouse click.

Two days later the bus from Bologna airport drops me at Piazzale della Libertà and heat immediately irons the wrinkles out of my jeans. The air smells of grain fields and burnt sugar. The city is terracotta lace threaded with rush hour Vespas. I walk Via Emilia and count balconies the way Protestant sailors once counted rosaries. Every corner advertises ceramic shops: windows of dishes, espresso cups, ornate toilets—yes, Faenza boldly commits to its brand. None of the painted signs mention Dolori. I begin to think the shard was a hallucination sent to punish or rescue me.

I find the first mention on the third floor of Municipal Library. It sits inside a disintegrating 1908 monograph—three sentences and an out-of-focus halftone. Dolori stands frozen in bowler hat, one hinge-kneed leg cocked against a stone lion. His moustache looks downright satirical. The caption says simply: “le opere minori non hanno ricevuto il favore del pubblico contemporaneo.” I mouth the Italian like hard caramel. The librarian, a Delphi-forehead woman with purple streaks in her gray hair, notices. She leads me to a cabinet that smells like basement soap and removes a dusty folder tied with legal ribbon. Inside: sketches of a dinner plate that was never produced. On the back someone penciled, “troppe crepe per il mercato”—too many cracks for the market. The phrase is a necklace of bruises.

That evening I eat an entire pizza because grief metabolizes quickly in forty-degree heat. A street musician plays Vivaldi on electric violin and the tourists lean against the wall like prosciuttos considering their own salt. I consider mine: I came here to give the shard back, but the city has already returned a larger piece. Failure, cracked right across the center, beautiful precisely because it is leaking light.

In the Airbnb nonna leaves me a jar of apricot jam and a painted tile on the bedside table. The tile shows a crescent moon cradling a small boat. No signature except two letters faintly scratched: G.D. When I rub my thumb across the surface the glaze is cool, impatient. I place the shard beside it. They do not fit; dimensions, color, era—nothing lines up. Yet together they look like a single sentence interrupted by punctuation from different grammars. That night I dream in dialect. The ceramic moon breaks open and cliffs of fire spill out, but the boat rows calmly, unlit, through falling stars made of biscuit porcelain. In the boat sits my mother at age thirty-two (exactly the passport photo age) waving a blue scarf. She yells something I cannot hear over gastric furnace of dream, but her eyes are wild with forgiveness.

Morning brings small mercies. I walk to the artisan district and ask about a workshop painted yellow since 1879. The cooper who on Via Chiappini tells me the building lies in the part of the city used as Allied quarters in 1945; much of it was eaten by mortar. He lends me a bicycle so battered I suspect it remembers Allied cigarettes. I pedal past palazzi sliced in half, rooftop gardens now open-air theaters. Kids play futsal on what used to be second floor. At noon I reach a dead-end alley smelling of wet clay and engine oil. One terracotta arch remains like a surprised mouth. In the weeds stands kiln brick as red as old blood. There is no plaque, only the cosmic silence of places where nothing official ever happened. I kneel, remove the shard, and gently tuck it into a nest of broken tile. When I press the earth around it the gesture feels both criminal and necessary, like burying the gun after the duel. Behind me a cat watches with emerald certainty, then yawns at human melodrama.

I stay an extra two nights in Faenza without knowing why. The nonna keeps feeding me parmigiano rinds and stories about la guerra. One afternoon she gives me a half-finished bowl glazed blue inside. The missing shard does not match the curve, but when I test it—holding my erstwhile pilgrim against the bowl—it rests like a depressed puzzle piece taking a cigarette break. She shrugs the shrug of masons: sometimes things break to complete each other’s circles.

Months later back in my own kitchen, the bowl now sits on the windowsill. Each sunrise throws another rivers of gold across the wall. It never runs through the same path twice; cracks keep telling you new things. I no longer stay up until 3:00 a.m. searching for Dolori. I stay up tracing instead the quiet interstice where loss stops being a scar and starts become a skylight. Every morning the crackles speak mostly in apology and gratitude, which overlap like warp and weft. The bowl has forgiven itself for breaking; the boy holding it is still practicing.

If Giuseppe Dolori still visits anybody, I hope he visits children who pocket shattered bottle glass believing the pieces are actually beach gems. I hope he tells them how color survives the firing, how stories scab over, how even a fragment speaks a whole sentence if you are brave enough to listen in the bluebell hush before engines wake. And should he be too busy, I volunteer as understudy: I will carry the shard’s sermon wherever people misplace their nights, whispering perdono until we are all safely cracked and open to the dawn.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 1000 words.
